narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 41.8% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to raise concerns about your readiness. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 4—below the typical student's 5.71—and especially your Year 3 decile of 2, which is much lower than the average of 5.53. Your LSAT score of 32 also falls short of the usual 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 is under the dataset average of 3.21. While your upper-middle family income aligns reasonably with peers, and as a full-time White male applicant you're in the majority group, these didn't offset the concerns. The strongest influence pushing the model toward predicting bar failure was your Year 3 performance, as it deviated most sharply from what's typical for successful students. Next in impact was your LSAT score, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, then family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and finally your full-time status. Each of these factors nudged the prediction toward failure rather than success, with lower-than-average academics carrying the most weight compared to typical students who pass the bar. You're not far off in probability terms—many applicants hover around here—but the model prioritizes patterns from past students where similar profiles struggled. To improve your odds moving forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward or above 36.7, as that's a key changeable factor. Strengthening your overall academic profile through additional coursework or certifications could help mimic higher deciles in future evaluations. Consider part-time options if full-time work might distract from studies, though that's a smaller lever. With targeted prep, students like you have turned similar profiles around for bar success—keep pushing, and reapply after addressing these standouts.
